[File: 'src/SableRewarder.sol -> Scope: Critical Reward extraction or unfair reward access'] [Function: issueSABLE / increaseF_SABLE] Can an unprivileged staker who calls SableStakingV2.stake() when totalSableLPStaked == 0 trigger sableRewarder.issueSABLE() which transfers SABLE tokens to SableStakingV2 and calls increaseF_SABLE(_SABLEGain), but because the guard `if (totalSableLPStaked > 0)` in increaseF_SABLE causes SABLEGainPerSABLEStaked to be set to 0, F_SABLE is never incremented, leaving the transferred SABLE permanently unclaimable in SableStakingV2 while the first staker who arrives after the zero-stake period captures a disproportionate share of future issuances relative to the SABLE already emitted, violating the invariant that every issued SABLE must be claimable by eligible stakers proportional to their stake? Local proof idea: unit test with latestRewardPerSec=1e18, advance time 1000s with totalSableLPStaked=0, then stake 1 LP, advance time 1s, unstake and assert SABLEGain < latestRewardPerSec*1001 but SableStakingV2.sableToken.balanceOf(SableStakingV2) > claimable.

[File: 'src/SableRewarder.sol -> Scope: Critical Reward extraction or unfair reward access'] [Function: issueSABLE / increaseF_SABLE] Can an unprivileged attacker who is the sole staker call SableStakingV2.unstake(0) repeatedly in rapid succession to trigger sableRewarder.issueSABLE() multiple times per block, and if the rewarder's issueSABLE uses block.timestamp without a same-block guard (unlike CommunityIssuance which sets lastIssuanceTime=block.timestamp), cause duplicate issuance for the same time interval, inflating F_SABLE and allowing the attacker to claim more SABLE than latestRewardPerSec * elapsed_seconds, violating the invariant that total SABLE distributed <= latestRewardPerSec * total_elapsed_time? Local proof idea: fuzz test calling unstake(0) N times in same block, assert totalSABLEIssued <= latestRewardPerSec * (block.timestamp - deploymentTime).

[File: 'src/SableRewarder.sol -> Scope: Critical Reward extraction or unfair reward access'] [Function: stake / issueSABLE / increaseF_SABLE] Can an unprivileged attacker with a large existing stake call SableStakingV2.stake(1) to trigger sableRewarder.issueSABLE() which updates F_SABLE, then immediately call unstake(0) in the same transaction to collect the just-issued SABLE gain, effectively harvesting rewards on demand without waiting for any protocol-defined distribution interval, bypassing any timing or eligibility rules that the rewarder was intended to enforce, violating the invariant that reward claims must respect the protocol's distribution schedule? Local proof idea: unit test stake(largeAmount), advance time T, call stake(1) then unstake(0) in same block, assert SABLEGain == latestRewardPerSec * T * largeAmount / totalSableLPStaked.

[File: 'src/SableRewarder.sol -> Scope: Critical Reward extraction or unfair reward access'] [Function: stake / _updateUserSnapshots / increaseF_SABLE] Can an unprivileged attacker exploit the ordering in SableStakingV2.stake() where sableRewarder.issueSABLE() is called first (updating F_SABLE via increaseF_SABLE), then _updateUserSnapshots captures the post-issuance F_SABLE, then the stake is increased, such that a new staker with currentStake==0 gets their snapshot set to the post-issuance F_SABLE and correctly earns zero past rewards, but an existing staker who calls stake() gets their accumulated gains computed against the post-issuance F_SABLE (including the just-issued SABLE) before the snapshot is reset, allowing them to claim the full current-period issuance even if they staked only 1 wei of LP, violating the invariant that reward entitlement is proportional to stake-weighted time? Local proof idea: invariant test with two stakers A (large) and B (tiny), assert B's SABLEGain/A's SABLEGain == B's stake/A's stake over same period.

[File: 'src/SableRewarder.sol -> Scope: Critical Reward extraction or unfair reward access'] [Function: increaseF_SABLE / _getPendingSABLEGain] Can an unprivileged attacker with a tiny stake (1 wei LP) exploit precision loss in the F_SABLE accumulator where SABLEGainPerSABLEStaked = _SABLEGain.mul(DECIMAL_PRECISION).div(totalSableLPStaked) truncates to 0 when _SABLEGain * 1e18 < totalSableLPStaked, causing F_SABLE to not increase despite SABLE being transferred to SableStakingV2, and then after other stakers withdraw (reducing totalSableLPStaked), trigger a new issuance where the same SABLE amount now produces a non-zero SABLEGainPerSABLEStaked, allowing the attacker to claim rewards that were supposed to be distributed in the earlier period, violating the invariant that precision loss must not create claimable surplus for any staker? Local proof idea: fuzz test with totalSableLPStaked in [1e18, 1e36], _SABLEGain in [1, 1e18], assert sum of all pending gains <= actual SABLE transferred to SableStakingV2.

[File: 'src/SableRewarder.sol -> Scope: Critical Reward extraction or unfair reward access'] [Function: issueSABLE / updateRewardPerSec] Can an unprivileged staker who observes a pending updateRewardPerSec transaction front-run it by calling SableStakingV2.stake(largeAmount) to trigger issueSABLE() at the old (lower) rate, then after updateRewardPerSec takes effect, call unstake(0) to collect rewards at the new (higher) rate for the period since their stake, effectively claiming rewards at a rate that was not in effect when they staked, violating the invariant that reward rate changes must not retroactively benefit stakers who entered before the change? Local proof idea: unit test stake at rate R1, advance time T1, updateRewardPerSec to R2, advance time T2, unstake and assert SABLEGain == R1*T1*stake/total + R2*T2*stake/total.

[File: 'src/SableRewarder.sol -> Scope: Critical Reward extraction or unfair reward access'] [Function: updateRewardPerSec] Can an unprivileged staker exploit the absence of a same-block issuance requirement in SableRewarder.updateRewardPerSec (unlike CommunityIssuance which requires lastIssuanceTime == block.timestamp after calling ownerTriggerIssuance) to allow the owner to change latestRewardPerSec without first settling the current period's rewards, causing the new rate to be applied retroactively to the time since lastIssuanceTime, so that when the next issueSABLE() is called the issuance = newRate * (now - lastIssuanceTime) instead of oldRate * elapsed + newRate * 0, allowing stakers to receive more or fewer SABLE than their entitlement, violating the invariant that rate changes must only affect future issuances? Local proof idea: unit test advance time T, updateRewardPerSec(newRate), advance time 0, call issueSABLE and assert issuance == newRate*T not oldRate*T.

[File: 'src/SableRewarder.sol -> Scope: Critical Reward extraction or unfair reward access'] [Function: issueSABLE / balanceSABLE] Can an unprivileged staker trigger SableStakingV2.stake() or unstake() which calls sableRewarder.issueSABLE(), and if the rewarder's SABLE balance (balanceSABLE()) is less than the computed issuance amount (latestRewardPerSec * timeSinceLastIssue), cause the SABLE transfer to SableStakingV2 to fail, reverting the entire stake/unstake transaction and permanently blocking all stakers from staking or unstaking until the rewarder is refunded, while the attacker who drained the rewarder's balance earlier (via a separate path) has already extracted their rewards, violating the invariant that reward issuance must not block principal withdrawal? Local proof idea: unit test drain rewarder SABLE balance, then call unstake and assert it reverts, proving principal is locked.

[File: 'src/SableRewarder.sol -> Scope: Critical Reward extraction or unfair reward access'] [Function: stake / _sendBNBGainToUser] Can an unprivileged attacker deploy a malicious receiver contract that reenters SableStakingV2.stake(1) from its receive() fallback triggered by _sendBNBGainToUser in the outer stake() call, and because in the reentrant call currentStake is already the post-outer-stake value (stakes[attacker] was increased before the BNB send), the reentrant call computes gains = currentStake * (F_SABLE - snapshot) / DECIMAL_PRECISION where snapshot was already updated, so gains = 0, but the reentrant call still calls sableRewarder.issueSABLE() again and increases the stake further, allowing the attacker to compound their stake position without paying LP tokens if the LP token's transferFrom is not re-checked, violating the invariant that stake increases must always be backed by LP token transfers? Local proof idea: unit test with malicious receiver that reenters stake(1) from receive(), assert sableLPToken.balanceOf(SableStakingV2) == stakes[attacker] after all calls.

[File: 'src/SableRewarder.sol -> Scope: Critical Reward extraction or unfair reward access'] [Function: unstake / _sendBNBGainToUser] Can an unprivileged attacker deploy a malicious receiver contract that reenters SableStakingV2.unstake(0) from its receive() fallback triggered by _sendBNBGainToUser, and because stakes[attacker] was already set to 0 (or reduced) before the BNB send, the reentrant unstake(0) call fails at _requireUserHasStake(0), but if the attacker reenters unstake(currentStake) instead (with a non-zero amount), and the snapshot was already updated but the stake was not yet reduced in a different code path, allow double-claiming of BNB gains, violating the invariant that BNB gains can only be claimed once per accumulation period? Local proof idea: unit test with malicious receiver that reenters unstake(originalStake) from receive(), assert total BNBGain received <= actual BNB in SableStakingV2.

[File: 'src/SableRewarder.sol -> Scope: Critical Reward extraction or unfair reward access'] [Function: stake / sableLPToken.transferFrom] Can an unprivileged attacker use an ERC777-compatible or callback-enabled LP token where transferFrom triggers a tokensToSend or tokensReceived hook, allowing the attacker to reenter SableStakingV2.stake() or unstake() after stakes[attacker] and totalSableLPStaked have been updated but before the LP tokens are actually transferred, creating a state where stakes[attacker] > actual LP held by SableStakingV2, and then call unstake(fullStake) to withdraw more LP than was deposited, violating the invariant that sableLPToken.balanceOf(SableStakingV2) >= totalSableLPStaked at all times? Local proof idea: unit test with ERC777 LP token mock that reenters unstake(fullStake) from tokensToSend hook, assert LP balance invariant after all calls.

[File: 'src/SableRewarder.sol -> Scope: Critical Reward extraction or unfair reward access'] [Function: increaseF_SABLE / stake / unstake] Can an unprivileged attacker who is the only staker call SableStakingV2.unstake(totalStake - 1) to reduce totalSableLPStaked to 1 wei, wait for a large time period to accumulate rewards, then call stake(largeAmount) which triggers issueSABLE() computing SABLEGainPerSABLEStaked = largeIssuance * 1e18 / 1 = largeIssuance * 1e18, causing F_SABLE to increase by an astronomically large value, and then immediately unstake to claim gains = 1 * (F_SABLE_new - F_SABLE_old) / 1e18 = largeIssuance, while other stakers who join after this point have their gains computed against the inflated F_SABLE baseline, receiving zero rewards for the period, violating the invariant that all stakers with equal stake-weighted time receive equal rewards? Local proof idea: invariant test with attacker reducing stake to 1 wei, advance time, new staker joins, assert new staker's gain == 0 while attacker claimed full issuance.

[File: 'src/SableRewarder.sol -> Scope: Critical Reward extraction or unfair reward access'] [Function: stake / increaseF_SABLE] Can an unprivileged attacker front-run another user's stake() transaction by calling stake(largeAmount) first to trigger issueSABLE() and capture the accumulated rewards at the current totalSableLPStaked, then the victim's stake() also triggers issueSABLE() but with timeSinceLastIssue=0 (same block), so the victim gets zero SABLE from the current period, and the attacker then unstakes to collect the full period's rewards, violating the invariant that rewards must be distributed proportionally to all stakers present during the accumulation period? Local proof idea: unit test with two txs in same block, assert victim's SABLEGain == 0 while attacker captured full period.

[File: 'src/SableRewarder.sol -> Scope: Critical Reward extraction or unfair reward access'] [Function: stake / _updateUserSnapshots] Can an unprivileged attacker exploit the fact that in SableStakingV2.stake(), when currentStake == 0, gains are NOT collected (the `if (currentStake != 0)` block is skipped) but _updateUserSnapshots IS called, so if the attacker previously had a stake, fully unstaked (setting stakes[attacker]=0 and updating snapshot to F_SABLE_at_unstake), then F_SABLE increases further, and the attacker re-stakes, the snapshot is updated to the new F_SABLE, permanently forfeiting the gains that accrued between unstake and re-stake, but if the attacker instead calls unstake(0) before re-staking (which requires currentStake > 0 and would fail), is there any path where the attacker can claim the between-period gains that were forfeited, violating the invariant that no SABLE issued during a period with zero stakers is claimable? Local proof idea: unit test full unstake, advance time, re-stake, assert no gains from the zero-stake period are claimable.

[File: 'src/SableRewarder.sol -> Scope: Critical Reward extraction or unfair reward access'] [Function: issueSABLE / latestRewardPerSec] Can an unprivileged staker exploit the linear unbounded emission model in SableRewarder (issuance = latestRewardPerSec * timeSinceLastIssue) where if the rewarder is not called for a very long time (e.g., years), the computed issuance vastly exceeds the rewarder's actual SABLE balance, causing the SABLE transfer to fail and reverting all stake/unstake operations, permanently locking all staked LP tokens in SableStakingV2 since unstake() requires issueSABLE() to succeed, violating the invariant that stakers must always be able to withdraw their principal? Local proof idea: unit test advance time by 10 years with latestRewardPerSec=1e18, assert unstake() reverts when rewarder balance < latestRewardPerSec * elapsed.

[File: 'src/SableRewarder.sol -> Scope: Critical Reward extraction or unfair reward access'] [Function: increaseF_SABLE / _getPendingSABLEGain] Can an unprivileged attacker exploit integer truncation in _getPendingSABLEGain where SABLEGain = stakes[user].mul(F_SABLE.sub(F_SABLE_Snapshot)).div(DECIMAL_PRECISION) truncates fractional SABLE, and by calling stake()/unstake() in rapid succession across many small time intervals, accumulate truncation dust that is never distributed to any staker, causing the sum of all pending gains to be less than the total SABLE transferred to SableStakingV2, and then exploit the fact that the contract has no sweep function to recover this dust, violating the invariant that sableToken.balanceOf(SableStakingV2) == sum of all pending gains + sum of all unclaimed gains? Local proof idea: fuzz test with N stakers and M time intervals, assert sum(pendingGains) + sum(claimedGains) == totalSABLETransferred.

[File: 'src/SableRewarder.sol -> Scope: Critical Reward extraction or unfair reward access'] [Function: increaseF_SABLE / increaseF_BNB / increaseF_USDS] Can an unprivileged attacker who is the only staker call SableStakingV2.unstake(totalStake) to set totalSableLPStaked=0, then trigger a BNB fee via TroveManager (which calls increaseF_BNB) or a USDS fee via BorrowerOperations (which calls increaseF_USDS) while totalSableLPStaked==0, causing BNBFeePerSABLEStaked=0 and USDSFeePerSABLEStaked=0 so F_BNB and F_USDS don't increase, and the fee BNB/USDS is sent to SableStakingV2 but never distributed, then the attacker re-stakes and claims only future fees, while the fees collected during the zero-stake window are permanently stuck, violating the invariant that all fees sent to SableStakingV2 must be claimable by eligible stakers? Local proof idea: unit test unstake all, trigger increaseF_BNB(1e18), re-stake, assert F_BNB unchanged and BNB stuck in contract.

[File: 'src/SableRewarder.sol -> Scope: Critical Reward extraction or unfair reward access'] [Function: stake / unstake / issueSABLE] Can an unprivileged attacker perform a sandwich attack by calling stake(largeAmount) in the same block as a large BNB fee event (increaseF_BNB), where the attacker's stake() triggers issueSABLE() first (updating F_SABLE), then the attacker's snapshot captures the current F_BNB (before the fee), and then the fee arrives via increaseF_BNB, and the attacker immediately calls unstake(0) to collect the BNB gain computed as largeAmount * (F_BNB_after - F_BNB_before) / DECIMAL_PRECISION, capturing a disproportionate share of the fee relative to their stake duration, violating the invariant that fee distribution must be proportional to stake-weighted time? Local proof idea: unit test stake in same block as increaseF_BNB, assert attacker's BNBGain == largeAmount * fee / totalSableLPStaked.

[File: 'src/SableRewarder.sol -> Scope: Critical Reward extraction or unfair reward access'] [Function: setParams / issueSABLE] Can an unprivileged observer exploit the fact that SableRewarder.setParams() sets lastIssuanceTime at deployment/initialization time, and if there is a long delay between deployment and setParams() being called (or between setParams and the first stake), the first call to issueSABLE() computes issuance = latestRewardPerSec * (now - lastIssuanceTime) for the entire pre-initialization period, minting a large SABLE amount that is distributed to whoever stakes first, allowing the first staker to claim rewards for a period when no staking was possible, violating the invariant that SABLE rewards must only accrue during periods when staking is active? Local proof idea: unit test deploy rewarder, wait T seconds, setParams, stake(1), advance 1s, unstake and assert SABLEGain includes T*latestRewardPerSec.

[File: 'src/SableRewarder.sol -> Scope: Critical Reward extraction or unfair reward access'] [Function: issueSABLE / increaseF_SABLE] Can an unprivileged attacker who monitors the mempool call SableStakingV2.stake(1) immediately before a large issueSABLE() trigger (e.g., another user's stake/unstake), so that the attacker's tiny 1-wei stake is included in totalSableLPStaked when increaseF_SABLE is called, giving the attacker SABLEGain = 1 * largeIssuance / totalSableLPStaked, and then immediately unstake(1) to collect this gain, effectively extracting SABLE rewards proportional to their stake fraction without any meaningful stake duration, violating the invariant that reward entitlement requires stake duration proportional to the accumulation period? Local proof idea: unit test attacker stakes 1 wei, victim triggers issueSABLE, attacker unstakes, assert attacker's gain == 1 * issuance / (total + 1).

[File: 'src/SableRewarder.sol -> Scope: Critical Reward extraction or unfair reward access'] [Function: stake / unstake / F_SABLE] Can an unprivileged attacker perform repeated stake()/unstake() cycles where each cycle triggers issueSABLE() twice (once per call), and if the rewarder does not enforce a minimum time between issuances, the attacker can drain the rewarder's SABLE balance faster than intended by triggering issuance at every block, while other stakers who do not call stake/unstake receive their rewards only when they next interact, creating an unfair distribution where active callers receive more frequent issuances than passive stakers, violating the invariant that reward distribution rate must be independent of call frequency? Local proof idea: fuzz test with attacker calling stake(1)/unstake(0) every block for N blocks, assert totalSABLEIssued == latestRewardPerSec * N_blocks_elapsed.

[File: 'src/SableRewarder.sol -> Scope: Critical Reward extraction or unfair reward access'] [Function: increaseF_SABLE / _getPendingSABLEGain] Can an unprivileged attacker exploit the fact that F_SABLE is a global accumulator that never resets, and if F_SABLE grows very large (due to many small totalSableLPStaked values over time), the computation stakes[user].mul(F_SABLE.sub(F_SABLE_Snapshot)).div(DECIMAL_PRECISION) could overflow uint256 for a user with a large stake and a very old snapshot, causing the pending gain calculation to revert and permanently locking the user's rewards, while the attacker who caused the F_SABLE inflation (by repeatedly staking tiny amounts) can still claim their own rewards, violating the invariant that all stakers must be able to claim their pending rewards? Local proof idea: fuzz test with totalSableLPStaked=1 for 1000 issuances, assert _getPendingSABLEGain does not revert for any staker.

[File: 'src/SableRewarder.sol -> Scope: Critical Reward extraction or unfair reward access'] [Function: stake / unstake / snapshots] Can an unprivileged attacker exploit the snapshot mechanism where _updateUserSnapshots sets F_SABLE_Snapshot = F_SABLE at the time of stake/unstake, and if the attacker calls stake() and unstake() in the same transaction (or same block), the snapshot is updated twice to the same F_SABLE value, so the attacker's pending gain for that period is 0, but the attacker has still triggered issueSABLE() twice, potentially causing the rewarder to issue SABLE for a zero-time interval (timeSinceLastIssue=0, issuance=0), wasting gas but not extracting value, unless the rewarder has a bug where timeSinceLastIssue is computed incorrectly? Local proof idea: unit test stake and unstake in same block, assert SABLEGain == 0 and totalSABLEIssued unchanged.

[File: 'src/SableRewarder.sol -> Scope: Critical Reward extraction or unfair reward access'] [Function: issueSABLE / SABLEToken._requireValidRecipient] Can an unprivileged attacker exploit the SABLEToken transfer restriction in _requireValidRecipient where transfers to sableStakingAddress are blocked unless msg.sender == sableRewarderAddress, and if the SableRewarder transfers SABLE to SableStakingV2 (which is sableStakingAddress), this transfer is allowed only because msg.sender == sableRewarderAddress, but if the rewarder's address is not correctly set in SABLEToken (sableRewarderAddress is immutable set at constructor), a misconfigured rewarder would cause all issueSABLE() calls to revert, blocking all stake/unstake operations and locking LP tokens, violating the invariant that stakers must always be able to withdraw their principal? Local proof idea: unit test deploy SABLEToken with wrong rewarder address, assert issueSABLE() reverts.

[File: 'src/SableRewarder.sol -> Scope: Critical Reward extraction or unfair reward access'] [Function: increaseF_SABLE / totalSableLPStaked] Can an unprivileged attacker exploit a race condition where they call SableStakingV2.unstake(largeAmount) to reduce totalSableLPStaked just before another user's stake() triggers issueSABLE(), so that the issuance is distributed over a smaller totalSableLPStaked, inflating F_SABLE per unit, and the attacker's remaining tiny stake receives a disproportionately large SABLEGain per LP token compared to what they would have received if they had not unstaked, then the attacker re-stakes to capture future rewards at the normal rate, violating the invariant that a staker cannot increase their per-unit reward by temporarily reducing their stake? Local proof idea: unit test attacker unstakes 99% of stake, victim triggers issueSABLE, attacker re-stakes, assert attacker's total gain > proportional entitlement.
